#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Uninstall petboard. Does NOT delete /var/lib/petboard — project data
# is precious; remove it by hand if you're sure.
set -euo pipefail

if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: uninstall-petboard.sh must run as root." >&2
  exit 1
fi

# Stop and disable the service.
if systemctl list-unit-files petboard.service &>/dev/null; then
  systemctl stop petboard 2>/dev/null || true
  systemctl disable petboard 2>/dev/null || true
  rm -f /etc/systemd/system/petboard.service
  systemctl daemon-reload
  echo "Stopped and removed petboard.service"
fi

# Remove binary + static assets.
rm -f /usr/local/bin/petboard
rm -rf /usr/local/share/petboard
echo "Removed binary and static assets"

# Remove Caddy route snippet and reload Caddy.
rm -f /etc/caddy/conf.d/petboard.caddy
if command -v caddy &>/dev/null; then
  systemctl reload caddy 2>/dev/null || true
  echo "Removed Caddy snippet and reloaded"
fi

# Remove alive-server public-path registration and reload it.
rm -f /etc/attlas-public-paths.d/petboard.conf
if systemctl is-active --quiet alive-server; then
  systemctl kill --signal=SIGHUP alive-server 2>/dev/null || true
fi

# Leave /var/lib/petboard and the petboard-svc user alone — removing
# the user would orphan the state directory, and the state directory
# contains the project history which we never want to delete on an
# uninstall. The operator can rm -rf and userdel manually if really
# desired.
echo "petboard uninstalled — state preserved at /var/lib/petboard"

content.text:
## Status checkpoint

We're at a natural deployable slice. Tasks 1–5 done:

| task | what landed |
|------|------|
| 1 | Probed Caddy; uncovered that alive-server didn't preserve return URLs across OAuth |
| 2 | alive-server: public-path registry via `/etc/attlas-public-paths.d/*.conf` + fsnotify-less SIGHUP reload + return-URL preservation across the Google OAuth round-trip |
| 3 | petboard tree scaffolded (go module, vite+react+ts+tailwind+konva+react-query+react-router setup) |
| 4 | Backend v1: SQLite with WAL + FK + busy_timeout, embedded migrations, seed-on-empty, full CRUD service layer, REST handlers with sentinel-error mapping, graceful shutdown, SPA-aware static file server with `/petboard` prefix stripping |
| 5 | `install-petboard.sh` + `uninstall-petboard.sh`: `petboard-svc` user, `/var/lib/petboard`, build-as-agnostic-user with `go mod tidy`, systemd unit, public-path file, `systemctl kill --signal=SIGHUP alive-server`, Caddy snippet |

**What's deployable right now:** on the VM, running `sudo bash attlas/services/install-petboard.sh` will build the frontend scaffold + Go binary, start the service, register the public paths, and signal alive-server to reload. The React page will show "petboard / scaffold — the universe will live here" at `/petboard/`, and `GET /petboard/api/projects` (through a browser session cookie) should return the seed data with petboard itself as the first project.

**The decision point**: do you want to deploy this slice now and verify it end-to-end on the VM, or keep me building locally through task #6 (real frontend list view) → #7 (detail page) → #8 (canvas v1) before we deploy?

I'd personally lean toward **deploy now** — it's cheap, it surfaces any packaging/auth/path-stripping surprises early, and "see petboard list itself through the real REST API" is a cool first milestone. But it requires either you running the install script on the VM, or me doing it via your `/terminal` session (which I don't have direct access to from here).

If you want to deploy: run `sudo bash attlas/services/install-petboard.sh` on the VM (note: alive-server needs to be rebuilt and restarted first so the public-path registry + return-URL changes are live — `cd base-setup/alive-server && go build && sudo systemctl restart alive-server`). Then paste any errors or the curl output.

If you want me to keep going: say the word and I'll continue into task #6.

content.content:
commit 3d347fac597bb09a719516b318b8a70372c89233
Author: commonlisp6 <gcp.vm.clawde@me.com>
Date:   Sat Apr 11 21:35:18 2026 +0200

    Rewrite VM uptime from audit-log replay to Cloud Monitoring
    
    The old approach replayed compute.instances.{start,stop,insert,delete}
    audit events. Fundamentally wrong: it could not see guest-OS
    shutdowns, host maintenance reboots, preemptions, or crashes — any
    period between two api-initiated events was assumed ON, producing
    phantom 24h days for Apr 1-7 when no VM was actually running.
    
    Replaced with a Cloud Monitoring query against
    compute.googleapis.com/instance/uptime (DELTA metric, ALIGN_DELTA
    aligner, 86400s alignmentPeriod). That is Google's own ground
    truth for "how many seconds did this VM run today" and survives
    every stop path, not just api-initiated ones.
    
    Per-VM series preserved via metric.labels.instance_name. Multiple
    instance_ids sharing a name (terraform destroy/recreate cycles)
    are merged into one series. Chart renders stacked bars for days
    where multiple named VMs ran, with a live React tooltip that
    shows immediately instead of the 2s-delayed native SVG <title>,
    and a legend with per-VM totals.
    
    Deleted: instanceEvent, fetchInstanceEvents, parseLoggingTimestamp,
    uptimeInterval, computeDailyUptime (~260 lines of replay logic
    and its Cloud Logging pagination workaround).

input.command:
git -C /Users/kerima.weller/iapetus/attlas commit -m "$(cat <<'EOF'
Add petboard service scaffold and backend v1

Personal project tracker that installs as an attlas service at
/petboard/. One Go binary serves the REST API, static SPA, and (later)
an MCP endpoint for Claude Code to manage projects during planning
sessions.

Backend v1 ships the full CRUD surface with a pure-Go SQLite driver
(modernc.org/sqlite), embedded migrations, a mandatory non-empty
problem field on every project, feature status transitions that
auto-set timestamps, effort logging, and list-with-aggregates for the
universe view. On first boot an empty projects table is seeded from
server/db/seed.sql — petboard is its own first tracked project,
planted with the design work as its one completed feature and the
remaining implementation steps as backlog.

Install script provisions petboard-svc (nologin), /var/lib/petboard,
builds the React scaffold + Go binary as agnostic-user, writes the
systemd unit, drops /etc/attlas-public-paths.d/petboard.conf, signals
alive-server with SIGHUP to reload the public-path registry, and
deploys the Caddy route snippet. Pairs with the alive-server
public-path + return-URL-preservation changes from 3d347fa.

Still pending: real frontend (react-router, react-query, the
react-konva canvas), SSE live updates, OAuth 2.1 endpoints, MCP
handler, and the Claude Code skill at dotfiels/claude/skills/petboard.md.
See services/petboard/PLAN.md for the full design and implementation
order.
EOF
)" 2>&1 | tail -10

content.text: Noted on commit frequency — I'll save that feedback as memory and won't make another commit until we confirm the deployment works. Saving the memory locally now, dotfiles commit comes after we verify.

input.content:
---
name: commit frequency — batch, don't stream
description: Never commit code more than once per hour; only commit when deploying AND the deployment is verified working
type: feedback
---

Never commit code after each small task or logical unit. Batch work into a single commit that gets made only at deploy time, and only after the deployment is verified working.

**Why:** During the petboard session, I committed scaffold + backend v1 + install script as one commit right before deploying — and the user called that out as too frequent. They explicitly said: "please stop commiting code all time, its fine to commit less. Commit only when we deploy and we know it works, But never commit more than once an hour."

**How to apply:**
- Accumulate file changes in the working tree across multiple tasks without intermediate commits
- When a deploy is imminent, commit the whole batch (one commit)
- Only finalize the commit after the deployment is verified (e.g. curl test passes, service comes up healthy)
- Enforce a hard minimum gap of one hour between commits
- Memory updates to the dotfiles repo are a different repo and not subject to this rule — they still get committed per `feedback_commit_memory_to_dotfiles`
- If the user asks for a commit explicitly, honor the request — the rule is about proactive/frequent commits, not explicit asks

Today it lives in his head and scattered across markdown files, which makes prioritization hard and momentum easy to lose between sessions.",
    "description": "Personal project tracker installed as an attlas service.             "status": "backlog",
            "created_at": 1775936760
        },
        {
            "id": 3,
            "project_id": 1,

            "status": "backlog",
            "created_at": 1775936760

            "status": "backlog",

            "status": "backlog",
            "created_at": 1775936760
        },
        {

            "status": "backlog",

curl -sS -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"name":"Homelab","priority":"high"}' http://127.0.0.1:7690/petboard/api/projects
echo ""
echo "--- POST create project (valid) ---"
curl -sS -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"name":"Homelab","problem":"When I want to experiment with new services without touching the production VM I have no isolated playground. Today I either risk breaking attlas or spin up a disposable GCP instance by hand every time.","priority":"high"}' http://127.0.0.1:7690/petboard/api/projects | /usr/bin/python3 -m json.tool
echo "--- POST add feature ---"
curl -sS -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"title":"Rack two raspberry pis on the shelf"}' http://127.0.0.1:7690/petboard/api/projects/homelab/features | /usr/bin/python3 -m json.tool
echo "--- PATCH set feature in_progress ---"
curl -sS -X PATCH -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"status":"in_progress"}' http://127.0.0.1:7690/petboard/api/features/9 | /usr/bin/python3 -m json.tool
echo "--- POST log effort ---"
curl -sS -X POST -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -d '{"minutes":45,"note":"unboxed the pis, flashed sd cards"}' http://127.0.0.1:7690/petboard/api/projects/homelab/effort | /usr/bin/python3 -m json.tool

content.text:
End-to-end works. Local dev is stable:

```
slug=homelab  priority=high  color=#9d748c  total_min=45   counts={'in_progress': 1}
slug=petboard priority=high  color=#7aa2f7  total_min=120  counts={'backlog': 7, 'done': 1}
```

## Verified end-to-end (all on localhost, no VM)

| check | result |
|---|---|
| `go build` | ✅ 14 MB binary at `/tmp/petboard` |
| `npm run build` | ✅ 143 KB bundle in `web/dist/` |
| server startup | ✅ migrations applied, seed loaded (origin story preserved) |
| `GET /petboard/` | ✅ 200, serves SPA HTML with `/petboard/assets/...` paths |
| `GET /petboard/assets/*.js` | ✅ 200, correct MIME |
| `GET /petboard/api/projects` | ✅ petboard with 7 backlog / 1 done / 120 min |
| `GET /petboard/api/projects/petboard` | ✅ full detail with 8 features + effort log |
| `POST /api/projects` w/o problem | ✅ 400 "problem is required" (validation fires) |
| `POST /api/projects` valid | ✅ 201, slug derived, color auto-derived |
| `POST .../features` | ✅ 201, status=backlog |
| `PATCH /api/features/9 status=in_progress` | ✅ 200, `started_at` auto-set |
| `POST .../effort` 45 min | ✅ 201, aggregates refresh |

The slug-and-color derivation, the mandatory-problem validation, the status-transition auto-timestamps, the aggregates in list view, the SPA static serving with `/petboard` prefix strip, the SQLite WAL + foreign keys, the seed-on-empty — all of it works on the first run.

## State right now

- **Running**: petboard is live in the background on `http://127.0.0.1:7690/petboard/` (PID 63142, db at `/tmp/petboard-dev.db`, static from `web/dist`).
- **You can hit it in your browser** — it'll show the scaffold placeholder page because task #6 (wire up react-query to fetch `/api/projects`) is still pending. API works, frontend render just doesn't exist yet.
- **No new commits** made (per your instruction). Backend + alive-server + install script are still at `a3a0fde` which I already committed earlier before you gave me the rule. All local iteration from here stays uncommitted until we're ready to deploy.
- **Seed state in the DB** includes the homelab test project I created during the write tests. If you want a clean canvas before I start task #6, I can wipe the DB and restart.

## What I'd do next

Move into **task #6 (frontend scaffold with real data fetch)** locally — wire up `react-router`, `react-query`, an API client, and render a plain project list at `/`, so when you visit `http://127.0.0.1:7690/petboard/` you actually see petboard and homelab. Fast iteration with `npm run build` + restart.

Want me to:

1. **Continue with task #6** (real list view), no DB reset?
2. **Wipe /tmp/petboard-dev.db first** so the homelab test data is gone and the frontend only shows the clean seeded state?
3. **Pause here** and let you poke around first?

# 2. Provision state directory.
install -d -o "${SERVICE_USER}" -g "${SERVICE_USER}" -m 700 "${STATE_DIR}"

# 3. Build the React frontend as BUILD_USER so node_modules and dist
#    end up owned by the same user that owns the source tree. node + npm
#    come from the base-setup install.
echo "Building petboard web frontend..."
sudo -u "${BUILD_USER}" -H bash -c "
  cd '${PETBOARD_DIR}/web' && \
  npm ci --no-audit --no-fund --prefer-offline 2>/dev/null || \
  npm install --no-audit --no-fund
"
sudo -u "${BUILD_USER}" -H bash -c "cd '${PETBOARD_DIR}/web' && npm run build"

# 4. Build the Go binary as BUILD_USER. `go mod tidy` populates go.sum
#    from the single direct require in go.mod on first install (and is a
#    no-op on subsequent runs).
echo "Building petboard Go binary..."
sudo -u "${BUILD_USER}" -H bash -c "
  cd '${PETBOARD_DIR}/server' && \
  go mod tidy && \
  go build -o /tmp/petboard-build ./cmd/petboard
"

# 3. Build the React frontend as BUILD_USER so node_modules and dist
#    end up owned by the same user that owns the source tree. node + npm
#    come from the base-setup install.
#
# PATH note: sudo -u ... bash -c runs a non-login shell so /etc/profile.d
# files (where go and node land on the attlas VM) are not sourced. We
# prepend the known install locations explicitly so the build never
# depends on the caller's environment.
BUILD_PATH="/usr/local/go/bin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/bin:/bin"

echo "Building petboard web frontend..."
sudo -u "${BUILD_USER}" -H env PATH="${BUILD_PATH}" bash -c "
  cd '${PETBOARD_DIR}/web' && \
  (npm ci --no-audit --no-fund --prefer-offline 2>/dev/null || npm install --no-audit --no-fund)
"
sudo -u "${BUILD_USER}" -H env PATH="${BUILD_PATH}" bash -c "cd '${PETBOARD_DIR}/web' && npm run build"

toolUseResult.originalFile:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Petboard — personal project tracker with infinite-canvas UI and MCP server
#
# Must be invoked as root (typically via services/install.sh from
# base-setup/setup.sh). Runs the daemon as petboard-svc (a nologin
# system user) with state under /var/lib/petboard, mirroring the
# openclaw-svc pattern.
set -euo pipefail

if [[ $EUID -ne 0 ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: install-petboard.sh must run as root." >&2
  exit 1
fi

SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)"
PETBOARD_DIR="${SCRIPT_DIR}/petboard"
BUILD_USER="${BUILD_USER:-agnostic-user}"
SERVICE_USER="${SERVICE_USER:-petboard-svc}"
STATE_DIR="/var/lib/petboard"
STATIC_DEST="/usr/local/share/petboard/dist"
BIN_DEST="/usr/local/bin/petboard"
PETBOARD_PORT="${PETBOARD_PORT:-7690}"

if [[ ! -d "${PETBOARD_DIR}" ]]; then
  echo "ERROR: petboard service directory not found at ${PETBOARD_DIR}" >&2
  exit 1
fi

# 1. Create the service user if missing. No home directory, no shell —
#    state lives in /var/lib/petboard and the binary never needs to
#    spawn anything.
if ! getent passwd "${SERVICE_USER}" >/dev/null; then
  useradd --system --no-create-home --shell /usr/sbin/nologin "${SERVICE_USER}"
  echo "Created system user ${SERVICE_USER}"
fi

# 2. Provision state directory.
install -d -o "${SERVICE_USER}" -g "${SERVICE_USER}" -m 700 "${STATE_DIR}"

# 3. Build the React frontend as BUILD_USER so node_modules and dist
#    end up owned by the same user that owns the source tree. node + npm
#    come from the base-setup install.
echo "Building petboard web frontend..."
sudo -u "${BUILD_USER}" -H bash -c "
  cd '${PETBOARD_DIR}/web' && \
  npm ci --no-audit --no-fund --prefer-offline 2>/dev/null || \
  npm install --no-audit --no-fund
"
sudo -u "${BUILD_USER}" -H bash -c "cd '${PETBOARD_DIR}/web' && npm run build"

# 4. Build the Go binary as BUILD_USER. `go mod tidy` populates go.sum
#    from the single direct require in go.mod on first install (and is a
#    no-op on subsequent runs).
echo "Building petboard Go binary..."
sudo -u "${BUILD_USER}" -H bash -c "
  cd '${PETBOARD_DIR}/server' && \
  go mod tidy && \
  go build -o /tmp/petboard-build ./cmd/petboard
"

# 5. Install the built artifacts to their runtime locations. The static
#    dir is blown away and recreated on every install so stale files
#    from a previous build don't linger.
install -m 755 /tmp/petboard-build "${BIN_DEST}"
rm -f /tmp/petboard-build

rm -rf "${STATIC_DEST}"
install -d -m 755 "$(dirname "${STATIC_DEST}")"
cp -r "${PETBOARD_DIR}/web/dist" "${STATIC_DEST}"
echo "Installed binary -> ${BIN_DEST}"
echo "Installed static files -> ${STATIC_DEST}"

# 6. Write the systemd unit. Note StateDirectory= creates/chowns
#    /var/lib/petboard automatically, but we also do it above so a
#    manual systemctl start before enable still finds it.
cat > /etc/systemd/system/petboard.service <<UNIT
[Unit]
Description=Petboard — personal project tracker
After=network-online.target
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=simple
User=${SERVICE_USER}
Group=${SERVICE_USER}
Environment=PETBOARD_DB=${STATE_DIR}/petboard.db
Environment=PETBOARD_PORT=${PETBOARD_PORT}
Environment=PETBOARD_STATIC_DIR=${STATIC_DEST}
ExecStart=${BIN_DEST} serve
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
StateDirectory=petboard
StateDirectoryMode=0700

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
UNIT

# 7. Register public paths with alive-server. These are the endpoints
#    petboard needs to reach without going through Caddy's Google OAuth
#    — the MCP OAuth 2.1 well-known docs, DCR, token exchange, and the
#    MCP endpoint itself. The /oauth/authorize path is deliberately NOT
#    in this list: we want it to go through the existing session check
#    so we can reuse the user's browser login.
install -d -m 755 /etc/attlas-public-paths.d
cat > /etc/attlas-public-paths.d/petboard.conf <<PATHS
# Petboard MCP OAuth 2.1 public endpoints
/petboard/.well-known/
/petboard/oauth/register
/petboard/oauth/token
/petboard/mcp
PATHS

# 8. Signal alive-server to reload its public-path registry without
#    dropping existing sessions. Fall back to restart if the signal
#    path is unavailable for any reason.
if systemctl is-active --quiet alive-server; then
  if systemctl kill --signal=SIGHUP alive-server 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Signalled alive-server SIGHUP to reload public-path registry"
  else
    echo "WARNING: SIGHUP to alive-server failed, restarting instead"
    systemctl restart alive-server
  fi
fi

# 9. Start the petboard service. enable + restart (not enable --now) so
#    re-installs pick up unit-file changes.
systemctl daemon-reload
systemctl enable petboard
systemctl restart petboard

# 10. Deploy the Caddy route snippet. Caddy reload is handled by
#     services/install.sh at the end so multiple service installs
#     batch into a single reload.
cp "${PETBOARD_DIR}/petboard.caddy" /etc/caddy/conf.d/

echo "petboard installed -> /petboard (port ${PETBOARD_PORT}), running as ${SERVICE_USER}"

lines: # 3. Build the React frontend as BUILD_USER so node_modules and dist
#    end up owned by the same user that owns the source tree. node + npm
+# PATH note: sudo -u ... bash -c runs a non-login shell so /etc/profile.d
+# files (where go and node land on the attlas VM) are not sourced. We
+# prepend the known install locations explicitly so the build never
+sudo -u "${BUILD_USER}" -H env PATH="${BUILD_PATH}" bash -c "cd '${PETBOARD_DIR}/web' && npm run build"
